name: Total Cholesterol
description: Measures the total amount of cholesterol in your blood, including LDL, HDL, and VLDL.
whyItMatters: High total cholesterol increases the risk of cardiovascular disease. For people with familial hypercholesterolemia (FH), this value is typically elevated from birth and requires ongoing monitoring.

name: LDL Cholesterol
description: Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol — the primary carrier of cholesterol to tissues. Often called 'bad' cholesterol because elevated levels drive atherosclerotic plaque formation.
whyItMatters: LDL is the single most important lipid marker for cardiovascular risk, especially in FH. Treatment targets vary: general population <3.0 mmol/L, high risk <1.8 mmol/L, very high risk <1.4 mmol/L.

name: HDL Cholesterol
description: High-density lipoprotein cholesterol — transports cholesterol away from arteries back to the liver. Often called 'good' cholesterol.
whyItMatters: Higher HDL is generally protective against cardiovascular disease. Low HDL (<1.0 mmol/L for men, <1.3 for women) is an independent risk factor.

name: Triglycerides
description: The most common type of fat in the blood. Your body converts excess calories into triglycerides and stores them in fat cells.
whyItMatters: Elevated triglycerides (>1.7 mmol/L) are associated with increased cardiovascular risk and can indicate metabolic syndrome. Very high levels (>10 mmol/L) risk pancreatitis.

name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
description: Total cholesterol minus HDL cholesterol. Captures all atherogenic lipoproteins (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)) in a single number.
whyItMatters: Increasingly seen as a better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone because it includes all 'bad' cholesterol types. Target is typically 0.8 mmol/L above your LDL target.

name: Lipoprotein(a)
description: A genetically determined lipoprotein particle similar to LDL but with an additional apolipoprotein(a) protein attached. Levels are ~90% determined by genetics.
whyItMatters: Elevated Lp(a) (>50 nmol/L or >30 mg/dL) is an independent, causal risk factor for cardiovascular disease. It cannot be significantly lowered by lifestyle changes.

name: Apolipoprotein B
description: A protein found on the surface of all atherogenic lipoprotein particles (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)). Each particle has exactly one ApoB molecule.
whyItMatters: Many experts consider ApoB the best single measure of cardiovascular risk because it counts atherogenic particles directly. Target: <0.9 g/L (general), <0.7 g/L (high risk).

name: VLDL Cholesterol
description: Very low-density lipoprotein cholesterol — carries triglycerides from the liver to tissues. Typically estimated as triglycerides divided by 5 (in mg/dL).
whyItMatters: Elevated VLDL contributes to atherosclerosis. Usually correlates with high triglycerides and metabolic dysfunction.

name: Total Cholesterol/HDL Ratio
description: The ratio of total cholesterol to HDL cholesterol. A simple measure of atherogenic risk.
whyItMatters: A ratio below 4.5 is desirable. Higher ratios indicate proportionally more atherogenic cholesterol relative to protective HDL.

name: Haemoglobin
description: The oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells. Measured as concentration in blood.
whyItMatters: Low haemoglobin indicates anaemia, which can cause fatigue, shortness of breath, and poor exercise tolerance. High values may indicate polycythaemia.

name: Haematocrit
description: The proportion of blood volume occupied by red blood cells, expressed as a fraction or percentage.
whyItMatters: Reflects red blood cell mass. Low values suggest anaemia; high values may indicate dehydration or polycythaemia.

name: Red Blood Cells
description: The number of red blood cells per litre of blood.
whyItMatters: Low counts indicate anaemia; high counts may suggest polycythaemia, dehydration, or chronic hypoxia.

name: Mean Cell Volume
description: The average volume of a single red blood cell. Helps classify anaemia by cell size (microcytic, normocytic, macrocytic).
whyItMatters: Low MCV suggests iron deficiency or thalassaemia; high MCV suggests B12/folate deficiency, liver disease, or hypothyroidism.

name: Mean Cell Haemoglobin
description: The average amount of haemoglobin per red blood cell.
whyItMatters: Low MCH correlates with iron deficiency; high MCH with B12/folate deficiency. Interpreted alongside MCV.

name: Mean Cell Haemoglobin Concentration
description: The average concentration of haemoglobin in each red blood cell.
whyItMatters: Low MCHC indicates hypochromic cells (e.g., iron deficiency); elevated MCHC can suggest spherocytosis.

name: Red Cell Distribution Width
description: A measure of variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). Expressed as coefficient of variation (%).
whyItMatters: Elevated RDW suggests mixed-population red cells — common in iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, or early response to treatment.

name: Platelets
description: Small cell fragments essential for blood clotting.
whyItMatters: Low platelets (thrombocytopenia) increase bleeding risk; high platelets (thrombocytosis) may indicate inflammation, infection, or myeloproliferative disorders.

name: Mean Platelet Volume
description: The average size of platelets. Larger platelets are often younger and more metabolically active.
whyItMatters: High MPV can indicate increased platelet turnover; low MPV may suggest bone marrow suppression.

name: White Blood Cells
description: The total number of white blood cells — the immune system's frontline defenders.
whyItMatters: Elevated WBC (leukocytosis) often indicates infection or inflammation. Low WBC (leukopenia) can signal bone marrow problems, autoimmune disease, or viral infection.

name: Neutrophils
description: The most abundant white blood cell type, first responders to bacterial infection.
whyItMatters: High neutrophils (neutrophilia) suggest bacterial infection or inflammation. Low neutrophils (neutropenia) increase infection risk — especially relevant with certain medications.

name: Lymphocytes
description: White blood cells responsible for adaptive immunity (T cells, B cells, NK cells).
whyItMatters: High lymphocytes (lymphocytosis) suggest viral infection or lymphoproliferative disorders. Low lymphocytes can indicate immunodeficiency.

name: Monocytes
description: White blood cells that mature into macrophages in tissues, involved in pathogen clearance and tissue repair.
whyItMatters: Elevated monocytes (monocytosis) can indicate chronic infection, inflammation, or haematological malignancy.

name: Eosinophils
description: White blood cells involved in fighting parasitic infections and in allergic responses.
whyItMatters: Elevated eosinophils (eosinophilia) suggest allergies, parasitic infection, drug reactions, or eosinophilic disorders.

name: Basophils
description: The rarest white blood cell type, involved in allergic and inflammatory reactions by releasing histamine.
whyItMatters: Usually present in very small numbers. Elevated basophils can indicate allergic reactions, chronic inflammation, or myeloproliferative disorders.

name: Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate
description: The rate at which red blood cells settle in a tube over one hour. A non-specific marker of inflammation.
whyItMatters: Elevated ESR suggests inflammation, infection, or autoimmune disease. Useful for monitoring conditions like polymyalgia rheumatica or temporal arteritis.

name: Reticulocytes
description: Immature red blood cells recently released from bone marrow. Indicates the rate of red cell production.
whyItMatters: High reticulocytes indicate active bone marrow response (e.g., after bleeding or haemolysis). Low values with anaemia suggest inadequate bone marrow response.

name: Sodium
description: The major cation in extracellular fluid, essential for fluid balance, nerve conduction, and muscle contraction.
whyItMatters: Low sodium (hyponatraemia) can cause confusion, seizures. High sodium (hypernatraemia) usually indicates dehydration.

name: Potassium
description: The major intracellular cation, critical for heart rhythm, muscle function, and nerve signalling.
whyItMatters: Both high and low potassium are dangerous — they can cause cardiac arrhythmias. Monitored with diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and kidney disease.

name: Chloride
description: A major anion in extracellular fluid, works with sodium to maintain fluid balance and acid-base equilibrium.
whyItMatters: Abnormal chloride often mirrors sodium abnormalities. Also important in diagnosing acid-base disorders.

name: Bicarbonate
description: The primary buffer in blood, maintaining pH. Reflects the metabolic component of acid-base balance.
whyItMatters: Low bicarbonate suggests metabolic acidosis (e.g., diabetic ketoacidosis, renal failure). High bicarbonate indicates metabolic alkalosis.

name: Anion Gap
description: The difference between measured cations (Na+, K+) and anions (Cl-, HCO3-). Helps classify causes of metabolic acidosis.
whyItMatters: A high anion gap suggests presence of unmeasured acids (lactic acid, ketoacids, toxins). Normal gap with acidosis points to bicarbonate loss.

name: Urea
description: A waste product from protein metabolism, filtered by the kidneys. Elevated levels indicate impaired kidney function or high protein intake/catabolism.
whyItMatters: Used alongside creatinine to assess kidney function. Elevated urea with normal creatinine may indicate dehydration or GI bleeding.

name: Creatinine
description: A waste product from muscle metabolism, filtered by the kidneys at a relatively constant rate.
whyItMatters: The most commonly used marker of kidney function. Elevated creatinine indicates reduced glomerular filtration. Used to calculate eGFR.

name: eGFR
description: An estimate of how well the kidneys filter waste, calculated from creatinine, age, sex, and (sometimes) ethnicity.
whyItMatters: The best overall indicator of kidney function. eGFR <60 sustained for 3+ months defines chronic kidney disease. Stages range from mild (60-89) to kidney failure (<15).

name: Uric Acid
description: The end product of purine metabolism. Elevated levels can crystallise in joints causing gout.
whyItMatters: High uric acid (hyperuricaemia) causes gout and is associated with kidney stones, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic syndrome.

name: Cystatin C
description: A protein produced by all nucleated cells at a constant rate, filtered by the kidneys. An alternative to creatinine for estimating kidney function.
whyItMatters: Less affected by muscle mass, diet, and age than creatinine, making it more accurate in elderly, very muscular, or malnourished patients.

name: Alanine Aminotransferase
description: An enzyme found primarily in the liver. Released into the blood when liver cells are damaged.
whyItMatters: Monitored in patients on statin therapy as statins can occasionally cause liver enzyme elevation. Persistently elevated ALT (>3x upper limit) may require dose adjustment.

name: Aspartate Aminotransferase
description: An enzyme found in the liver, heart, and muscles. Less liver-specific than ALT but still important for monitoring liver health.
whyItMatters: Like ALT, monitored during statin therapy. The AST/ALT ratio can help differentiate causes of liver enzyme elevation.

name: Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase
description: An enzyme found in the liver and bile ducts. Sensitive marker for liver and biliary disease.
whyItMatters: Elevated GGT can indicate liver disease, bile duct problems, or alcohol use. Also increasingly recognised as an independent cardiovascular risk marker.

name: Alkaline Phosphatase
description: An enzyme found in the liver, bones, kidneys, and intestines. Elevated levels can indicate liver or bone disease.
whyItMatters: High ALP with high GGT suggests liver/biliary origin. High ALP with normal GGT suggests bone origin (e.g., Paget's disease, growing children).

name: Bilirubin (Total)
description: A yellow pigment produced from the breakdown of red blood cells, processed by the liver and excreted in bile.
whyItMatters: Elevated bilirubin causes jaundice. Can indicate liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or increased red cell breakdown (haemolysis). Gilbert's syndrome causes benign mild elevation.

name: Bilirubin (Direct)
description: The water-soluble form of bilirubin that has been conjugated by the liver. Elevated levels specifically suggest liver or biliary disease.
whyItMatters: High direct bilirubin with normal indirect suggests biliary obstruction. Helps differentiate pre-hepatic, hepatic, and post-hepatic causes of jaundice.

name: Total Protein
description: The sum of albumin and globulins in the blood.
whyItMatters: Low total protein can indicate liver disease, malnutrition, or protein-losing conditions. High levels may suggest chronic inflammation or multiple myeloma.

name: Albumin
description: The most abundant protein in blood, produced by the liver. Maintains oncotic pressure and transports hormones, drugs, and fatty acids.
whyItMatters: Low albumin indicates liver disease, malnutrition, inflammation, or kidney loss (nephrotic syndrome). A marker of overall health status.

name: Globulin
description: A group of proteins including immunoglobulins (antibodies) and transport proteins. Calculated as total protein minus albumin.
whyItMatters: Elevated globulin can indicate chronic infection, inflammation, or immune disorders. Low levels may suggest immunodeficiency.

name: Albumin/Globulin Ratio
description: The ratio of albumin to globulin. Helps identify protein imbalances.
whyItMatters: Low A/G ratio can indicate liver disease, kidney disease, or chronic inflammation. Useful when total protein appears normal but individual components are abnormal.

name: Lactate Dehydrogenase
description: An enzyme found in nearly all body tissues. Released when cells are damaged.
whyItMatters: Non-specific marker of tissue damage. Elevated in haemolysis, liver disease, heart attack, muscle injury, some cancers, and pulmonary embolism.

name: Creatine Kinase
description: An enzyme found mainly in the heart, brain, and skeletal muscle. Released when muscle tissue is damaged.
whyItMatters: Critical to monitor on statin therapy. Statins can rarely cause myopathy (muscle damage). CK >10x upper limit with muscle symptoms may indicate rhabdomyolysis.

name: Calcium
description: Essential for bone health, muscle contraction, nerve function, and blood clotting. Most is stored in bones; blood levels are tightly regulated.
whyItMatters: High calcium (hypercalcaemia) can indicate hyperparathyroidism, cancer, or vitamin D excess. Low calcium causes muscle cramps, tetany, and cardiac issues.

name: Corrected Calcium
description: Calcium adjusted for albumin levels. Since ~40% of blood calcium is bound to albumin, low albumin falsely lowers total calcium.
whyItMatters: More accurate than total calcium when albumin is abnormal. Used to determine true calcium status in patients with liver disease or malnutrition.

name: Phosphate
description: An essential mineral for bone structure, energy metabolism (ATP), and DNA/RNA synthesis.
whyItMatters: High phosphate (hyperphosphataemia) is common in kidney disease and accelerates vascular calcification. Low levels cause muscle weakness.

name: Magnesium
description: A mineral involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions including energy production, muscle function, and nerve signalling.
whyItMatters: Deficiency is very common and underdiagnosed. Low magnesium causes muscle cramps, arrhythmias, and worsens insulin resistance. Often depleted by proton pump inhibitors.

name: Zinc
description: An essential trace element involved in immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, and taste/smell.
whyItMatters: Deficiency impairs immune function, delays wound healing, and causes hair loss and skin lesions. Common in malabsorption and chronic disease.

name: Copper
description: An essential trace element needed for iron metabolism, connective tissue formation, and nerve function.
whyItMatters: Deficiency causes anaemia and neurological problems. Excess (Wilson's disease) causes liver and brain damage.

name: Iron
description: The amount of iron circulating in blood, bound to transferrin.
whyItMatters: Low iron suggests deficiency (common cause of anaemia). High iron can indicate haemochromatosis or iron overload.

name: Ferritin
description: The main iron storage protein. Blood ferritin reflects total body iron stores.
whyItMatters: The most sensitive test for iron deficiency. Low ferritin (<30 ug/L) confirms iron deficiency. Very high levels may indicate iron overload, inflammation, or liver disease.

name: Transferrin
description: The main iron transport protein in blood. Produced by the liver, it carries iron from the gut and storage sites to the bone marrow.
whyItMatters: High transferrin indicates iron deficiency (body making more transporters). Low transferrin occurs in liver disease, malnutrition, or chronic inflammation.

name: Total Iron Binding Capacity
description: The maximum amount of iron that transferrin can bind. Reflects the blood's capacity to transport iron.
whyItMatters: High TIBC indicates iron deficiency; low TIBC suggests iron overload or chronic disease.

name: Transferrin Saturation
description: The percentage of transferrin that is bound to iron (serum iron / TIBC × 100).
whyItMatters: Low saturation (<20%) confirms iron deficiency. High saturation (>45%) suggests iron overload (haemochromatosis screen).

name: Fasting Glucose
description: The concentration of glucose in your blood after fasting for at least 8 hours.
whyItMatters: Used to screen for and monitor diabetes and pre-diabetes. FH patients have a slightly increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes on high-intensity statin therapy.

name: HbA1c
description: Measures the percentage of haemoglobin with attached glucose, reflecting average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months.
whyItMatters: Gold standard for diabetes monitoring. Values 5.7-6.4% indicate pre-diabetes, ≥6.5% indicates diabetes. Monitored in statin patients as statins can marginally increase HbA1c.

name: Fasting Insulin
description: The level of insulin in the blood after fasting. Insulin is the hormone that regulates blood sugar.
whyItMatters: Elevated fasting insulin indicates insulin resistance — a precursor to type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome. Can be elevated years before glucose rises.

name: C-Peptide
description: A byproduct of insulin production, released in equal amounts to insulin. More stable than insulin itself for measurement.
whyItMatters: Helps distinguish type 1 (low C-peptide) from type 2 diabetes (normal/high C-peptide). Not affected by exogenous insulin injections.

name: Homocysteine
description: An amino acid in the blood. Elevated levels are associated with cardiovascular disease and are often caused by B12 or folate deficiency.
whyItMatters: High homocysteine is an independent cardiovascular risk factor. Usually treatable with B12, folate, and B6 supplementation.

name: High-Sensitivity CRP
description: A protein produced by the liver in response to inflammation. The high-sensitivity assay detects low-grade chronic inflammation relevant to cardiovascular risk.
whyItMatters: Elevated hsCRP (>3.0 mg/L) indicates systemic inflammation and is an independent predictor of cardiovascular events.

name: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone
description: Hormone produced by the pituitary gland that regulates thyroid function. The most sensitive screening test for thyroid disorders.
whyItMatters: Hypothyroidism is a common secondary cause of elevated cholesterol. Checking TSH ensures that high lipids aren't being driven by an underactive thyroid.

name: Free T4
description: The unbound, active form of the thyroid hormone thyroxine (T4). About 0.03% of total T4 is free.
whyItMatters: Low free T4 with high TSH confirms hypothyroidism. High free T4 with low TSH indicates hyperthyroidism.

name: Free T3
description: The unbound, active form of triiodothyronine (T3), the most metabolically potent thyroid hormone.
whyItMatters: Useful in diagnosing T3 thyrotoxicosis (where T4 is normal but T3 is elevated). Also helps assess thyroid conversion ability.

name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies
description: Autoantibodies against the thyroid enzyme peroxidase. A marker of autoimmune thyroid disease.
whyItMatters: Positive TPO antibodies indicate Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune hypothyroidism) or Graves' disease risk. Present in ~5-10% of the population.

name: Vitamin D
description: The storage form of vitamin D, measured to assess vitamin D status. Produced in skin from sunlight and obtained from diet/supplements.
whyItMatters: Deficiency is extremely common and linked to bone health, immune function, and cardiovascular risk. Generally >75 nmol/L (>30 ng/mL) is considered sufficient.

name: Vitamin B12
description: A water-soluble vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, nerve function, and red blood cell formation. Stored in the liver for years.
whyItMatters: Deficiency causes macrocytic anaemia and irreversible neurological damage if untreated. Common in vegetarians/vegans, elderly, and those on metformin or PPIs.

name: Folate
description: A B vitamin essential for DNA synthesis and cell division. Critical during pregnancy for neural tube development.
whyItMatters: Deficiency causes macrocytic anaemia similar to B12 deficiency. Also elevates homocysteine, increasing cardiovascular risk.

name: Vitamin A
description: A fat-soluble vitamin essential for vision, immune function, and cell growth.
whyItMatters: Deficiency causes night blindness and immune dysfunction. Excess is toxic (hepatotoxic). Most common in malabsorption and liver disease.

name: Vitamin E
description: A fat-soluble antioxidant vitamin that protects cell membranes from oxidative damage.
whyItMatters: Deficiency is rare but causes neurological problems. Levels should be interpreted relative to lipid levels (cholesterol-adjusted).

name: Cortisol
description: The primary stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands. Follows a diurnal pattern — highest in morning, lowest at night.
whyItMatters: High cortisol (Cushing's) causes weight gain, high blood sugar, and hypertension. Low cortisol (Addison's) causes fatigue, weight loss, and can be life-threatening.

name: Testosterone (Total)
description: The primary male sex hormone, also present in females at lower levels. Important for muscle mass, bone density, and reproductive function.
whyItMatters: Low testosterone causes fatigue, low libido, muscle loss, and osteoporosis. Statins may slightly reduce testosterone levels.

name: Sex Hormone Binding Globulin
description: A protein that binds and transports sex hormones (testosterone and estradiol) in the blood, regulating their bioavailability.
whyItMatters: High SHBG reduces free (active) testosterone. Low SHBG is associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

name: Estradiol
description: The most potent form of oestrogen. Important for reproductive function, bone health, and cardiovascular protection.
whyItMatters: In premenopausal women, low estradiol causes menstrual irregularity and bone loss. In postmenopausal women, low levels increase cardiovascular risk.

name: Follicle Stimulating Hormone
description: A pituitary hormone that stimulates egg follicle development in women and sperm production in men.
whyItMatters: Elevated FSH in women suggests ovarian failure or menopause. In men, high FSH with low testosterone indicates primary testicular failure.

name: Luteinising Hormone
description: A pituitary hormone that triggers ovulation in women and stimulates testosterone production in men.
whyItMatters: LH/FSH ratio is important in diagnosing polycystic ovarian syndrome. Elevated LH in men may indicate primary hypogonadism.

name: Prolactin
description: A hormone produced by the pituitary gland, primarily responsible for stimulating milk production.
whyItMatters: Elevated prolactin (hyperprolactinaemia) can cause menstrual irregularity, infertility, and galactorrhoea. May indicate a pituitary adenoma.

name: DHEA-Sulphate
description: An adrenal androgen precursor. The most abundant circulating steroid hormone.
whyItMatters: Elevated DHEA-S in women may indicate adrenal androgen excess (e.g., PCOS, adrenal tumour). Declines with age in both sexes.

name: Parathyroid Hormone
description: A hormone produced by the parathyroid glands that regulates calcium and phosphate levels.
whyItMatters: High PTH with high calcium indicates primary hyperparathyroidism. High PTH with low calcium is secondary (e.g., vitamin D deficiency, kidney disease).

name: INR
description: A standardised measure of how long it takes blood to clot, based on the prothrombin time (PT). Used to monitor anticoagulant therapy.
whyItMatters: Target INR depends on indication: 2.0-3.0 for most conditions (e.g., atrial fibrillation), 2.5-3.5 for mechanical heart valves. High INR = higher bleeding risk.

name: Prothrombin Time
description: The time it takes plasma to clot after adding tissue factor. Tests the extrinsic and common coagulation pathways.
whyItMatters: Prolonged PT indicates deficiency of factors VII, X, V, II, or fibrinogen. Used alongside INR for warfarin monitoring.

name: Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time
description: A measure of the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways. Tests factors XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, II, and fibrinogen.
whyItMatters: Prolonged APTT suggests haemophilia, von Willebrand disease, or heparin therapy. Also used to monitor heparin dosing.

name: Fibrinogen
description: A protein produced by the liver that is converted to fibrin during clotting, forming the structural scaffold of blood clots.
whyItMatters: Low fibrinogen increases bleeding risk. High fibrinogen is an acute-phase reactant (elevated in inflammation) and an independent cardiovascular risk factor.

name: D-Dimer
description: A fibrin degradation product — released when blood clots are broken down. A marker of clot formation and dissolution.
whyItMatters: Elevated D-dimer suggests active clotting and fibrinolysis. Primarily used to rule out deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. Also elevated in infection, surgery, and pregnancy.

name: Troponin
description: A protein released from damaged heart muscle cells. The most sensitive and specific marker for myocardial injury.
whyItMatters: Elevated troponin indicates heart muscle damage — most importantly myocardial infarction (heart attack). Serial measurements distinguish acute from chronic elevation.

name: BNP
description: A hormone released by the heart ventricles in response to stretching from volume overload.
whyItMatters: Elevated BNP indicates heart failure. Used to diagnose, grade severity, and guide treatment of heart failure.

name: NT-proBNP
description: An inactive fragment released alongside BNP. Longer half-life makes it more stable for measurement than BNP.
whyItMatters: Used like BNP to diagnose and monitor heart failure. Age-adjusted cut-offs: <300 pg/mL rules out acute heart failure.

name: Prostate-Specific Antigen
description: A protein produced by the prostate gland. Used as a screening and monitoring tool for prostate cancer.
whyItMatters: PSA >4.0 ng/mL may warrant further investigation, but is not specific to cancer — BPH, prostatitis, and age also raise PSA.

name: Carcinoembryonic Antigen
description: A protein found in some cancers, especially colorectal cancer. Also elevated in smokers and some benign conditions.
whyItMatters: Not used for screening but for monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence of colorectal and other cancers.

name: CA-125
description: A protein marker associated with ovarian cancer. Also elevated in endometriosis, pregnancy, and liver disease.
whyItMatters: Used primarily to monitor ovarian cancer treatment response and detect recurrence. Not recommended for routine screening.

name: Alpha-Fetoprotein
description: A protein normally produced by the fetal liver. In adults, elevated levels suggest liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma) or germ cell tumours.
whyItMatters: Used to screen for liver cancer in high-risk patients (chronic hepatitis B/C, cirrhosis) and monitor germ cell tumour treatment.

name: Immunoglobulin A
description: An antibody class found primarily in mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, respiratory and GI tract). Also present in blood.
whyItMatters: Low IgA (selective IgA deficiency) is the most common primary immunodeficiency. High IgA may indicate IgA nephropathy, liver disease, or coeliac disease.

name: Immunoglobulin G
description: The most abundant antibody class in blood. Provides long-term immunity against bacteria and viruses.
whyItMatters: Low IgG increases infection risk. High IgG may indicate chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or multiple myeloma.

name: Immunoglobulin M
description: The first antibody produced in response to a new infection. Large molecule that stays mainly in blood.
whyItMatters: High IgM indicates acute or recent infection. Persistent elevation may suggest Waldenström's macroglobulinaemia or primary biliary cholangitis.

name: Complement C3
description: A key protein in the complement immune system, involved in pathogen clearance and inflammation.
whyItMatters: Low C3 suggests complement consumption (active lupus, glomerulonephritis, severe infection). Can help monitor autoimmune disease activity.

name: Complement C4
description: A complement protein in the classical pathway. Consumed during immune complex-mediated inflammation.
whyItMatters: Low C4 is seen in lupus flares and hereditary angioedema. Combined with C3, helps assess complement pathway activity.

name: Total IgE
description: An antibody associated with allergic responses and parasitic infections.
whyItMatters: Elevated IgE indicates allergic conditions (asthma, eczema, hay fever), parasitic infections, or some immune disorders.

name: Haemoglobin A
description: Normal adult haemoglobin. Should constitute >95% of total haemoglobin.
whyItMatters: Reduced HbA percentage may indicate haemoglobinopathy (e.g., sickle cell disease, thalassaemia) or increased levels of variant haemoglobins.

name: Haemoglobin A2
description: A minor component of adult haemoglobin (normally 2-3%). Elevated in beta-thalassaemia trait.
whyItMatters: HbA2 >3.5% is diagnostic of beta-thalassaemia trait — important for genetic counselling, especially in couples planning pregnancy.

name: Haemoglobin F
description: Fetal haemoglobin — predominant in newborns, normally <1% in adults.
whyItMatters: Elevated HbF in adults can indicate thalassaemia, sickle cell disease, or hereditary persistence of fetal haemoglobin.

name: Lipase
description: An enzyme produced by the pancreas that breaks down fats. More specific for pancreatic disease than amylase.
whyItMatters: Elevated lipase (>3x normal) strongly suggests acute pancreatitis. Also elevated in pancreatic cancer and kidney failure.

name: Amylase
description: An enzyme that breaks down starch. Produced by the pancreas and salivary glands.
whyItMatters: Elevated amylase suggests acute pancreatitis but is less specific than lipase (also elevated in salivary gland disease, bowel obstruction).

name: Urine Albumin/Creatinine Ratio
description: The ratio of albumin to creatinine in a spot urine sample. Screens for early kidney damage (microalbuminuria).
whyItMatters: Early detection of kidney damage, especially in diabetes and hypertension. Values >3 mg/mmol indicate albuminuria and increased cardiovascular risk.

name: Neutrophils (%)
description: The percentage of white blood cells that are neutrophils.
whyItMatters: Interpreted alongside absolute neutrophil count and total WBC for a complete picture of immune function.

name: Lymphocytes (%)
description: The percentage of white blood cells that are lymphocytes.
whyItMatters: High percentage may indicate viral infection; low percentage can suggest immunodeficiency.

name: Monocytes (%)
description: The percentage of white blood cells that are monocytes.
whyItMatters: Mildly elevated in chronic infections and autoimmune conditions.

name: Eosinophils (%)
description: The percentage of white blood cells that are eosinophils.
whyItMatters: Elevated percentage suggests allergic conditions or parasitic infections.

name: Basophils (%)
description: The percentage of white blood cells that are basophils.
whyItMatters: Rarely clinically significant on its own. Very high levels may indicate myeloproliferative disorder.
